title: Theory of the specialization of the fundamental group

pages.title: Theory of the specialization of the fundamental group
pages.html:
X Theory of the specialization of the fundamental group

In the present exposé, we limit ourselves to the study of the fundamental group of the geometric fibres in a proper morphism, i.e. of the fundamental group of a variable proper algebraic scheme. In a later exposé, we shall generalize the technique employed to the tamely ramified étale coverings “at infinity”. This will give us, for example, a solution of the “Problem of the three points” in the case of the Galois coverings of order prime to the characteristic (i.e. a determination of the Galois coverings of the line \(\mathbf{P}_k^1\), ramified in at most three given points and tamely ramified at these points), and of its obvious variants.

pages.title: The homotopy exact sequence for a proper and separable morphism
pages.html:
1 The homotopy exact sequence for a proper and separable morphism

Definition 1

A prescheme \(X\) over a field \(k\) is said to be separable, or separable over \(k\), if for every extension \(K\) of \(k\), \(X\otimes_k K\) is reduced. If \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is a morphism of preschemes, one says that \(f\) is separable, or that \(X\) is separable over \(Y\), if \(X\) is flat over \(Y\) and if for every \(y\in Y\), the fibre \(X\otimes_Y \mathit{k}(y)\) is separable over \(\mathit{k}(y)\).

If \(X\) is a prescheme over a field \(k\), to say that it is separable also means that it is reduced, and that the fields \(\mathit{k}(x)\), for \(x\) the generic point of an irreducible component of \(X\), are separable extensions of \(k\). If \(k\) is perfect, it therefore amounts to the same thing to say that \(X\) is reduced. Let us note that if \(X\) is separable over \(Y\), then for every base change \(Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), \(X'=X\times_Y Y'\) is separable over \(Y'\). One can also prove, under suitable finiteness hypotheses, that the composite of two separable morphisms is a separable morphism. We shall need this only in the following form: if \(X\) is separable over \(Y\), and \(X'\) étale over \(X\), then \(X'\) is separable over \(Y\). This is indeed an immediate consequence of the definitions and I 2. Moreover, the hypothesis “separable morphism” will be of use to us through the following proposition:

Proposition 2

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a proper and separable morphism, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian, and consider its Stein factorization \(X\xrightarrow{f'}Y'\displaystyle\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), (where \(f_*'(\mathscr{O}_X)=\mathscr{O}_{Y'}\), \(Y'\) being finite over \(Y\) and isomorphic to the spectrum of the algebra \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_X)\)). Then \(Y'\) is an étale covering of \(Y\).

This proposition will appear in (EGA III 7)1. Let us indicate the principle of the proof. One reduces easily to the case where \(Y\) is the spectrum of a complete local ring \(A\), and by making moreover a suitable finite flat extension of the latter (corresponding to a suitable residual extension), one may assume that the connected components of the fibre of the closed point \(y\) are geometrically connected, which also means that \(\mathrm{H}^0(X_y,\mathscr{O}_{X_y})\) decomposes into a product of fields identical to \(k=\mathit{k}(y)\). Then assuming \(X\) connected, which is permissible, we shall have \(\mathrm{H}^0(X_y,\mathscr{O}_{X_y})=k\), so the homomorphism \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(X_y,\mathscr{O}_{X_y})\) is surjective. One concludes by a general proposition (of Künneth type) that \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_X)\) is defined by a module \(B\) over \(A\) which is free over \(A\), and that \(B/\mathfrak{m} B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(X_y,\mathscr{O}_{X_y})=k\) is bijective. Therefore in this case \(B\) is an étale algebra over \(A\), which completes the proof.

Theorem 3

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a proper and separable morphism, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian and connected, and suppose \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_X)=\mathscr{O}_Y\) (which implies that the fibres of \(X\) over \(Y\) are geometrically connected, and conversely by virtue of 2). Let \(y\) be a point of \(Y\), \(\overline{\mathit{k}(y)}\) an algebraic closure of \(\mathit{k}(y)\), \(\overline X_y =X_y\otimes_{\mathit{k}(y)} \overline{\mathit{k}(y)}\). Let finally \(X'\) be a connected étale covering of \(X\), and \(\overline X_y' =X_y'\otimes_{\mathit{k}(y)} \overline{\mathit{k}(y)}\). In order that there exist an étale covering \(Y'\) of \(Y\) and an \(X\)-isomorphism \(X'\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X\times_Y Y'\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\overline{X'}_y\) admit a section over \(\overline X_y\).

Setting \(Y'=\operatorname{Spec}(h_*(\mathscr{O}_{X'}))\) (where \(h\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is the composite morphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\)), it suffices to prove that the canonical \(Y\)-morphism

\[ X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_Y Y' \]

is an isomorphism, and that \(Y'\) is étale over \(Y\). Now we already know by 2 that \(Y'\) is étale over \(Y\), so \(X\times_Y Y'\) is étale over \(X\), hence the morphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_Y Y'\) is likewise étale (I 8). Moreover, \(Y'\) is connected as the image of \(X'\), which is so, so \(X\times_Y Y'\) is connected since \(X\) has connected fibres over \(Y\) (IX 4 and V 9 (iii)). Thus to prove that \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_Y Y'\) is an isomorphism, it suffices to see that its projection degree at one point of \(X\times_Y Y'\) is equal to 1. Now this follows easily from the hypothesis that \(\overline{X'}_y\) admits a section over \(\overline X_y\), either by use of IX 6, or more simply by noting that it suffices to prove the existence of such a point in \(X\times_Y Y'\) after the base change \(\operatorname{Spec}(\overline{\mathit{k}(y)})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), where this is evident. This completes the proof of 3.

Taking into account IX 4 and the dictionary V 9 and V 11, one can put 3 into the following equivalent form

Corollary 4

With the preceding notations for \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) and \(\overline X_y\), let \(\overline{a}\) be a geometric point of \(\overline X_y\), \(a\) its image in \(X\) and \(b\) its image in \(Y\). Then the following sequence of homomorphisms of groups is exact:

\[ \pi_1(\overline X_y,\overline{a})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(Y,b)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e. \]
Remarks 5

One will note that the proof of 3 makes essential use of 2 and thereby of the “first comparison theorem” in formal-algebraic geometry. On the other hand, the descent theory of Exposé IX intervened only through IX 4, of which a direct proof in the case of a proper morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) such that \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_X)=\mathscr{O}_Y\) is easy. Indeed, let \(Y'\) be étale over \(Y\) and suppose that \(X' = X\times_Y Y'\) is a disjoint sum of two non-empty opens; let us prove that the same holds for \(Y'\). Indeed, we shall have \(Y'=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{A})\), so \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{B})\) with \(\mathscr{B}=\mathscr{A}\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_Y} \mathscr{O}_X\), and the decomposition of \(X'\) into a direct sum corresponds to a decomposition of \(\mathscr{B}\) into a product of two non-zero Algebras \(\mathscr{B}_1\) and \(\mathscr{B}_2\). Since \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_X)=\mathscr{O}_Y\) one concludes easily \(f_*(\mathscr{B})=\mathscr{A}\), so \(\mathscr{A}\) will be a sum of two Algebras (likewise non-zero, since their unit sections are non-zero) \(f_*(\mathscr{B}_1)\) and \(f_*(\mathscr{B}_2)\), q.e.d.

6

Let us again suppose that \(f\) is proper and separable, but let us no longer make any hypothesis on \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_X)\), which will correspond to a well-determined étale covering \(Y'\) of \(Y\), moreover pointed above \(b\) by the image \(b'\) of \(a\). Then applying 4 to the canonical morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\), and supposing \(f\) surjective, the exact sequence 4 is replaced by the following one, analogous to the homotopy exact sequence of fibre spaces in algebraic topology:

\[ \pi_1(\overline X_y,\overline a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(Y,b)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_0(\overline X_y,\overline a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_0(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_0(Y,b)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e. \]

Of course, in 4 one cannot in general assert that the homomorphism \(\pi_1(\overline X_y,\overline a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\) is injective; in algebraic topology, its kernel is the image of \(\pi_2(Y,b)\), and it would be appropriate in algebraic geometry as well to introduce homotopy groups in all dimensions, and the complete exact homotopy sequence for a proper morphism satisfying suitable hypotheses (for example that of being a smooth morphism). At the present time no result in this direction is available, with the exception of a reasonable (if not definitive) definition of the higher homotopy groups.

Corollary 7

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(X\) and \(Y\) two connected preschemes over \(k\); suppose \(X\) proper over \(k\) and \(Y\) locally Noetherian. Let \(a\) be a geometric point of \(X\), \(b\) a geometric point of \(Y\) with values in the same algebraically closed extension \(K\) of \(k\), consider the geometric point \(c=(a,b)\) of \(X\times_k Y\), and the homomorphism \(\pi_1(X\times_k Y,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\times\pi_1(Y,b)\) deduced from the homomorphisms on the fundamental groups associated with the two projections \(X\times_k Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) and \(X\times_k Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\). The preceding homomorphism is an isomorphism.

Suppose first that \(K=k\). Set \(Z=X\times_k Y\), consider the projection \(f\colon Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) and the locality \(y\) of the geometric point \(b\) of \(Y\), and apply to this situation the result 4. Note for this that, at the cost of passing to \(X_\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}\) (which does not change the fundamental groups under consideration), one may already suppose \(X\) reduced, hence separable over \(k\), so that \(Z\) is separable over \(k\), and obviously with geometrically connected fibres (since \(X\) is connected). The geometric fibre of \(Z\) at \(b\) is canonically isomorphic to \(X\otimes_k K=X\). On the other hand, since the composite of the morphisms \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is the identity, one finds that \(\pi_1(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(Z,c)\) is injective, and 1.4 gives us an exact sequence:

\[ e\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(Z,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(Y,b)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e. \]

On the other hand, we have the canonical exact sequence:

\[ e\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\times \pi_1(Y,b)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(Y,b)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e, \]

where the two homomorphisms written are the canonical injection and the canonical projection. We have, finally, a homomorphism from the first exact sequence into the second, by means of the identity morphisms on the extreme terms, and the canonical homomorphism \(\pi_1(Z,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\times\pi_1(Y,b)\) for the middle terms. The commutativity of the diagram thus obtained is verified trivially. Since the homomorphisms on the extreme terms are isomorphisms, the same holds for the middle terms, which proves 7 in this case.

When one no longer assumes \(K=k\), one finds only an isomorphism

\[ \pi_1(Z,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X\otimes_k K,a)\times \pi_1(Y,b), \]

and 7 then amounts to the following particular case:

Corollary 8

Let \(X\) be a proper and connected scheme over an algebraically closed field \(k\), \(k'\) an algebraically closed extension of \(k\), \(a'\) a geometric point of \(X\otimes_k k'\) and \(a\) its image in \(X\). Then the canonical homomorphism \(\pi_1(X\otimes_k k',a')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\) is an isomorphism.

The fact that this homomorphism is surjective amounts to saying that if \(X'\) is a connected étale covering of \(X\), then \(X'\otimes_k k'\) is likewise connected, and follows at once from the fact that \(k\) is algebraically closed; it is also a particular case of IX 4. The hypothesis of properness on \(X\) has not yet been used. This said, to say that the homomorphism under consideration is injective also means this: every étale covering of \(X\otimes_k k'\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of an étale covering of \(X\). It is essentially a matter of formalities that one can find a sub-\(k\)-algebra \(A\) of \(k'\), of finite type over \(k\), and an étale covering of \(X\otimes_k A\) whose inverse image on \(X\otimes_k k'\) is isomorphic to the given covering. So let \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), which is an integral \(k\)-scheme of finite type, hence has rational points over \(k\). Then apply 7 to the fundamental group of \(X\times Y\) at a point \((a,b)\) rational over \(k\): one finds that every connected étale covering of \(X\times Y\) is isomorphic to a quotient of a covering \(X'\times Y'\), where \(X'\), \(Y'\) are étale Galois coverings of \(X\) and \(Y\) with groups \(G\), \(G'\), by a subgroup \(H\) of \(G\times G'\). This implies that the inverse image of this covering of \(X\times Y\) on \(X\times Y'\) is isomorphic to a covering of the form \(X_1'\times Y'\), where \(X_1'\) is an étale covering of \(X\). If then \(L\) is the field of functions of \(Y\), equal to the field of fractions of \(A\) in \(k'\), the étale covering of \(X\otimes_k L\) induced by the given covering of \(X\times_k Y\) is such that there exists a finite separable extension \(L'\) of \(L\) such that the inverse image of the said covering on \(X\otimes_k L'\) is isomorphic to \(X_1'\otimes_k L'\). Now, since \(k'\) is algebraically closed, one may suppose that the extension \(L'\) of \(L\) is contained in \(k'\). This proves that the given étale covering of \(X\otimes_k k'\) is isomorphic to \(X_1'\otimes_k k'\), q.e.d.

The explicit form indicated in passing for the étale coverings of a product \(X\times_k Y\) immediately implies the following result:

Corollary 9

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(X\) and \(Y\) two locally Noetherian preschemes over \(k\), \(Z=X\times_k Y\) their product, \(Z'\) an étale covering of \(Z\). For every point \(y\in Y\) rational over \(k\), let \(i_y \colon \operatorname{Spec}(k)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be the canonically associated morphism, \(j_y = \mathrm{id}_X\times_k i_y\) the corresponding morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\). Finally let \(X_y'\) be the étale covering of \(X\) that is the inverse image of \(Z'\) by \(j_y\). Suppose \(Y\) connected, and \(X\) or \(Y\) proper over \(k\). Then the coverings \(X_y'\) of \(X\) are all isomorphic.

Figuratively, one may say that a family of étale coverings of \(X\), parametrized by a connected prescheme \(Y\), is constant if \(X\) or the parameter prescheme \(Y\) is proper over \(k\).

Remarks 10

Corollaries 7 to 9 are due to Lang-Serre [2] in the case of normal algebraic schemes. (Their work was the initial motivation for the theory of the fundamental group developed in this Seminar). As these authors have remarked, these results become inexact when one drops the hypothesis of properness, at least in characteristic \(p>0\). Taking for example for \(X\) the affine line \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(k[t])\), it is not difficult to see that the coverings of \(X\), parametrized by the affine line \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(k[s])\), defined by the equations

\[ x^p - x = st, \]

are étale and pairwise non-isomorphic. This puts 9 in default, and a fortiori 7, and one sees in the same way that if \(s\) is regarded as an element transcendental over \(k\) in an algebraically closed extension \(K\) of \(k\), one finds an étale covering \(X'\) of \(X\otimes_kK\) which does not come from an étale covering of \(X\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(X\) and \(Y\) two connected preschemes over \(k\); suppose \(X\) proper over \(k\) and \(Y\) locally Noetherian. Let \(a\) be a geometric point of \(X\), \(b\) a geometric point of \(Y\) with values in the same algebraically closed extension \(K\) of \(k\), consider the geometric point \(c=(a,b)\) of \(X\times_k Y\), and the homomorphism \(\pi_1(X\times_k Y,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\times\pi_1(Y,b)\) deduced from the homomorphisms on the fundamental groups associated with the two projections \(X\times_k Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) and \(X\times_k Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\). The preceding homomorphism is an isomorphism.

Suppose first that \(K=k\). Set \(Z=X\times_k Y\), consider the projection \(f\colon Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) and the locality \(y\) of the geometric point \(b\) of \(Y\), and apply to this situation the result 4. Note for this that, at the cost of passing to \(X_\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}\) (which does not change the fundamental groups under consideration), one may already suppose \(X\) reduced, hence separable over \(k\), so that \(Z\) is separable over \(k\), and obviously with geometrically connected fibres (since \(X\) is connected). The geometric fibre of \(Z\) at \(b\) is canonically isomorphic to \(X\otimes_k K=X\). On the other hand, since the composite of the morphisms \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is the identity, one finds that \(\pi_1(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(Z,c)\) is injective, and 1.4 gives us an exact sequence:

\[ e\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(Z,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(Y,b)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e. \]
On the other hand, we have the canonical exact sequence:

\[ e\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\times \pi_1(Y,b)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(Y,b)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e, \]

where the two homomorphisms written are the canonical injection and the canonical projection. We have, finally, a homomorphism from the first exact sequence into the second, by means of the identity morphisms on the extreme terms, and the canonical homomorphism \(\pi_1(Z,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\times\pi_1(Y,b)\) for the middle terms. The commutativity of the diagram thus obtained is verified trivially. Since the homomorphisms on the extreme terms are isomorphisms, the same holds for the middle terms, which proves 7 in this case.
When one no longer assumes \(K=k\), one finds only an isomorphism
\[ \pi_1(Z,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X\otimes_k K,a)\times \pi_1(Y,b), \]
and 7 then amounts to the following particular case:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8

Let \(X\) be a proper and connected scheme over an algebraically closed field \(k\), \(k'\) an algebraically closed extension of \(k\), \(a'\) a geometric point of \(X\otimes_k k'\) and \(a\) its image in \(X\). Then the canonical homomorphism \(\pi_1(X\otimes_k k',a')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\) is an isomorphism.

The fact that this homomorphism is surjective amounts to saying that if \(X'\) is a connected étale covering of \(X\), then \(X'\otimes_k k'\) is likewise connected, and follows at once from the fact that \(k\) is algebraically closed; it is also a particular case of IX 4. The hypothesis of properness on \(X\) has not yet been used. This said, to say that the homomorphism under consideration is injective also means this: every étale covering of \(X\otimes_k k'\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of an étale covering of \(X\). It is essentially a matter of formalities that one can find a sub-\(k\)-algebra \(A\) of \(k'\), of finite type over \(k\), and an étale covering of \(X\otimes_k A\) whose inverse image on \(X\otimes_k k'\) is isomorphic to the given covering. So let \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), which is an integral \(k\)-scheme of finite type, hence has rational points over \(k\). Then apply 7 to the fundamental group of \(X\times Y\) at a point \((a,b)\) rational over \(k\): one finds that every connected étale covering of \(X\times Y\) is isomorphic to a quotient of a covering \(X'\times Y'\), where \(X'\), \(Y'\) are étale Galois coverings of \(X\) and \(Y\) with groups \(G\), \(G'\), by a subgroup \(H\) of \(G\times G'\). This implies that the inverse image of this covering of \(X\times Y\) on \(X\times Y'\) is isomorphic to a covering of the form \(X_1'\times Y'\), where \(X_1'\) is an étale covering of \(X\). If then \(L\) is the field of functions of \(Y\), equal to the field of fractions of \(A\) in \(k'\), the étale covering of \(X\otimes_k L\) induced by the given covering of \(X\times_k Y\) is such that there exists a finite separable extension \(L'\) of \(L\) such that the inverse image of the said covering on \(X\otimes_k L'\) is isomorphic to \(X_1'\otimes_k L'\). Now, since \(k'\) is algebraically closed, one may suppose that the extension \(L'\) of \(L\) is contained in \(k'\). This proves that the given étale covering of \(X\otimes_k k'\) is isomorphic to \(X_1'\otimes_k k'\), q.e.d.
The explicit form indicated in passing for the étale coverings of a product \(X\times_k Y\) immediately implies the following result:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(X\) and \(Y\) two locally Noetherian preschemes over \(k\), \(Z=X\times_k Y\) their product, \(Z'\) an étale covering of \(Z\). For every point \(y\in Y\) rational over \(k\), let \(i_y \colon \operatorname{Spec}(k)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be the canonically associated morphism, \(j_y = \mathrm{id}_X\times_k i_y\) the corresponding morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\). Finally let \(X_y'\) be the étale covering of \(X\) that is the inverse image of \(Z'\) by \(j_y\). Suppose \(Y\) connected, and \(X\) or \(Y\) proper over \(k\). Then the coverings \(X_y'\) of \(X\) are all isomorphic.
Figuratively, one may say that a family of étale coverings of \(X\), parametrized by a connected prescheme \(Y\), is constant if \(X\) or the parameter prescheme \(Y\) is proper over \(k\).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 10

Corollaries 7 to 9 are due to Lang-Serre [2] in the case of normal algebraic schemes. (Their work was the initial motivation for the theory of the fundamental group developed in this Seminar). As these authors have remarked, these results become inexact when one drops the hypothesis of properness, at least in characteristic \(p>0\). Taking for example for \(X\) the affine line \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(k[t])\), it is not difficult to see that the coverings of \(X\), parametrized by the affine line \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(k[s])\), defined by the equations

\[ x^p - x = st, \]

are étale and pairwise non-isomorphic. This puts 9 in default, and a fortiori 7, and one sees in the same way that if \(s\) is regarded as an element transcendental over \(k\) in an algebraically closed extension \(K\) of \(k\), one finds an étale covering \(X'\) of \(X\otimes_kK\) which does not come from an étale covering of \(X\).

pages.title:
Application of the existence theorem for sheaves:
semi-continuity theorem for the
fundamental groups of the fibres of a proper and separable morphism
pages.html:
2 Application of the existence theorem for sheaves: semi-continuity theorem for the fundamental groups of the fibres of a proper and separable morphism

Theorem 1

Let \(Y\) be the spectrum of a complete Noetherian local ring, with residue field \(\mathit{k}\), \(X\) a proper \(Y\)-scheme, \(X_0=X\otimes_A \mathit{k}\), \(a_0\) a geometric point of \(X_0\) and \(a\) the corresponding geometric point of \(X\). Then the canonical homomorphism \(\pi_1(X_0,a_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\) is an isomorphism.

This is merely a translation, into the language of the fundamental group, of the result recalled in IX 10. It is here that the existence theorem for sheaves in formal-algebraic geometry enters in an essential way into the theory of the fundamental group.

Let us now introduce an algebraic closure \(\overline{k}\) of the residue field \(k\), and the geometric fibre \(\overline X_0 = X_0\otimes_k \overline{k}\). We thus have the exact sequence (IX 1)

\[ e\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(\overline X_0,\overline a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X_0,a_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(k,\overline{k})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e, \]

on the other hand we have the isomorphism 1 and the analogous, more elementary, isomorphism,

\[ \pi_1(k,\overline{k})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(Y,b), \]

where \(b\) is the image of \(a\) in \(Y\). One thus finds:

Corollary 2

With the preceding notations, suppose \(\overline X_0=X_0\otimes_k \overline{k}\) connected, and let \(\overline a_0\) be a geometric point of \(\overline X_0\), \(a_0\) its image in \(X\), \(b_0\) its image in \(Y\). Then the following sequence of canonical homomorphisms

\[ e\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(\overline X_0,\overline a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(Y,b_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e \]

is exact.

One will compare this sequence with the exact sequence 4, but one will note that a) one did not have to make any hypothesis of flatness, or of separability on the fibres, for \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\); b) one has the important complement that the morphism \(\pi_1(\overline X_0,\overline a_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a_0)\) is injective.

This last fact will allow us to compare the fundamental group of the other geometric fibres of \(X\) over \(Y\) with that of \(\overline X_0\). Indeed, let \(y_1\) be an arbitrary point of \(Y\), \(X_1\) its fibre and \(\overline X_1\) its geometric fibre, relative to an algebraically closed extension of \(\mathit{k}(y_1)\), \(\overline a_1\) a geometric point of \(\overline X_1\), \(a_1\) its image in \(X\) and \(b_1\) its image in \(Y\). Let us choose a “path class” from \(a_1\) to \(a_0\), whence a path class from \(b_1\) to \(b_0\), whence a commutative diagram of homomorphisms:

\[ \xymatrix@=.5cm{ &{\pi_1(\overline X_1,\overline a_1)}\ar[r]&{\pi_1(X,a_1)}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{\pi_1(Y,b_1)}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{e}\\{e}\ar[r]&{\pi_1(\overline X_0,\overline a_0)}\ar[r]&{\pi_1(X,a_0)}\ar[r]&{\pi_1(Y,b_0)}\ar[r]&{e,}} \]

where the two vertical arrows written are isomorphisms. Since the second row is exact, one thus finds a canonical homomorphism, which we shall call the specialization homomorphism for the fundamental group (depending only on the chosen path class from \(a_1\) to \(a_0\), hence defined modulo inner automorphism of \(\pi_1(X,a_0)\)):

\[ \pi_1(\overline X_1,\overline a_1)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(\overline X_0,\overline a_0). \]

When the first row above is likewise exact, it follows at once that the specialization homomorphism is surjective. One thus finds, taking 4 into account:

Corollary 3

Under the conditions of 1, suppose moreover that the morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is separable (1) and \(\overline X_0\) connected (so that by virtue of 2 one has \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_X)=\mathscr{O}_Y\)). Then for every geometric fibre \(\overline X_1\) of \(X\) over \(Y\), equipped with a geometric point \(\overline a_1\), the specialization homomorphism defined above is a surjective homomorphism.

This is a result of semi-continuity for the fundamental group, which does not yet seem to have an analogue in algebraic topology. One may moreover state it under more general conditions:

Corollary 4

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a proper morphism with geometrically connected fibres, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian, \(y_0\) and \(y_1\) two points of \(Y\) such that \(y_0\in\overline{\{y_1\}}\), \(\overline X_0\) and \(\overline X_1\) the geometric fibres of \(X\) corresponding to given algebraically closed extensions of \(\mathit{k}(y_0)\) and \(\mathit{k}(y_1)\), \(\overline a_0\) resp. \(\overline a_1\) a geometric point of \(\overline X_0\) resp. \(\overline X_1\). Then one can define in a natural way a specialization homomorphism:

\[ \pi_1(\overline X_1,\overline a_1)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(\overline X_0,\overline a_0), \]

defined up to inner automorphism, and this is a surjective homomorphism if \(f\) is a separable morphism (1).

Indeed, it follows first from 8 that 4 is essentially independent of the algebraically closed extensions chosen for the residue fields \(\mathit{k}(y_0)\) and \(\mathit{k}(y_1)\). This allows us to replace \(Y\) by a prescheme \(Y'\) over \(Y\) having a point \(y_0'\) (resp. \(y_1'\)) above \(y_0\) (resp. \(y_1\)). One will then take for \(Y'\) the spectrum of the completion of the local ring of \(y_0\) in \(Y\), and one applies 3.

Remarks 5

The final conclusion of 4 on the surjectivity of the specialization homomorphism, and a fortiori the results 3 and 4 of which it is a consequence, becomes inexact if one no longer assumes that \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is a separable morphism, even for schemes projective over an algebraically closed field of characteristic \(0\). We shall see examples of this later on, both in the case where \(f\) is flat but where \(f\) admits a non- separable fibre (\(X\) and \(Y\) being however smooth over \(k\)), and in the case where the fibres of \(f\) are indeed separable but where \(f\) is not flat (for example \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) being a birational morphism of normal integral schemes), cf. XI 3. In these examples, it may happen that the fundamental group of the generic geometric fibre is trivial, but not that of a suitable special geometric fibre. On the other hand, even if \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is a proper separable morphism as in 4, it commonly happens that the specialization morphism is not an isomorphism. Thus, it is easy to give examples where \(\overline X_1\) is a non-singular elliptic curve, (hence its fundamental group is commutative, and its \(\ell\)-primary component for a prime number \(\ell\) prime to the characteristic is isomorphic to \(\mathbf{Z}_\ell^2\), cf. XI), while \(\overline X_0\) consists either of two rational non-singular curves meeting at two points, or of two rational curves tangent at a point, or finally of a rational curve having a singular point which is a cusp. (For the complete classification of degenerate elliptic curves, see the recent works of Kodaira [1] and Néron). One then sees that in these cases, the fundamental group of \(\overline X_0\) is respectively \(\widehat{\mathbf{Z}}\), \(e\), \(e\), hence “strictly smaller” than that of \(\overline X_1\). We shall however see later, when \(f\) is a smooth morphism, a bound on the kernel of the homomorphism of specialization, which implies in particular that if \(\mathit{k}(y_0)\) is of characteristic 0, the specialization homomorphism is an isomorphism. But even for a smooth morphism, if the characteristic of \(\mathit{k}(y_0)\) is \(>0\), it may happen that the specialization homomorphism is not an isomorphism, as one sees for example in the case where \(X\) is an abelian scheme over \(Y\) (of relative dimension \(1\), if one wishes), cf. XI 2. A satisfactory theory of the specialization of the fundamental group must take account of the “continuous component” of the “true” fundamental group, corresponding to the classification of the principal coverings with structural group the infinitesimal groups; whereby one would be entitled to expect that the “true” fundamental groups of the geometric fibres of a smooth and proper morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) form a nice local system over \(X\), an inverse limit of finite and flat group schemes over \(X\)1. We shall return later to this point of view, our present aim being on the contrary to push as far as possible the phenomena common to the topological theory and the scheme-theoretic theory of the fundamental group.

Let now \(X_0\) be a proper, smooth and connected curve of genus \(g\) over an algebraically closed field \(k\). If \(k\) is of characteristic zero, its fundamental group can be determined by transcendental means in the following way. It is known that \(X_0\) arises by base extension from a curve defined over an algebraically closed extension of finite transcendence degree of the prime field \(\mathbf{Q}\), and taking 8 into account, one may assume that \(k\) is itself of finite transcendence degree over \(\mathbf{Q}\). One may therefore assume that \(k\) is a subfield of the field \(\mathbf{C}\) of complex numbers, and a further application of 8 allows us to assume that \(k=\mathbf{C}\). It is then not difficult to verify that the fundamental group of \(X\) is isomorphic to the compactification of the fundamental group of the associated topological space \(\widetilde{X}\) (a compact oriented surface of genus \(g\)), for the topology defined by the subgroups of finite index2. It is on the other hand classical that the topological fundamental group is generated by \(2g\) generators \(s_i,t_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant g\)), subject to a single relation:

\[ (s_1t_1s_1^{-1}t_1^{-1})\cdots(s_gt_gs_g^{-1}t_g^{-1})=1. \]

Hence the fundamental group of \(X\) admits \(2g\) topological generators \(s_i,t_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant g\)), linked by the single preceding relation. If now the characteristic of \(k\) is \(p>0\), let us denote by \(A\) the ring of Witt vectors constructed with \(k\), and by \(K\) an algebraically closed extension of its field of fractions. We saw in III 4 that there exists a scheme \(X\) over \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), proper and smooth over \(Y\), reducing to \(X_0\). Applying 3 to it, one finds a surjective morphism

\[ \pi_1(X_1)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X_0), \]

where \(X_1=X\otimes_A K\). It is immediate3 that \(X_1\) is smooth over \(K\), connected (2), of dimension \(1\), and its genus is equal to \(g\) (by the invariance of the Euler-Poincaré characteristic, cf. EGA III 7). Since \(K\) is of characteristic \(0\), one may apply to it the preceding result. We have thus proved by transcendental means:

Theorem 6

Let \(X_0\) be a smooth proper and connected algebraic curve over an algebraically closed field \(k\), and let \(g\) be its genus. Then \(\pi_1(X_0)\) admits a system of \(2g\) topological generators, linked by the relation written above. When the characteristic of \(k\) is \(0\), \(\pi_1(X_0)\) is even the free group of Galois type for the generators and the relation which precede.

Remarks 7

There does not exist at the present time, to the knowledge of the writer, any proof by purely algebraic means of the preceding result, (except for the genera \(0,1\)). To begin with, one hardly sees how to distinguish in \(\pi_1(X_0)\) \(2g\) elements, of which one might then expect that they form a system of topological generators. In this respect, the situation of the rational line deprived of \(n\) points, and the study of the coverings thereof tamely ramified at these points, is more agreeable, since the consideration of the ramification groups at these \(n\) points furnishes \(n\) elements of the fundamental group to be studied, of which one indeed shows that they generate topologically this fundamental group, as we shall see later on4. But even in this particularly concrete case, there does not seem to exist any purely algebraic proof. Such a proof would obviously be extremely interesting. The only fact concerning the fundamental group of a curve that one knows how to prove by purely algebraic means (with the exception of the weak finiteness theorem 12 below, proved by algebraic means by Lang-Serre [2]), seems to be the determination of the fundamental group made abelian via the Jacobian (indicated in IX 8 last line).

8

The last assertion of 6 is no longer valid in characteristic \(p>0\), as one already sees in the case of the elliptic curves. As we have already pointed out, we do not know whether the fundamental group of \(X_0\) is topologically of finite presentation; this seems altogether improbable.

Theorem 9

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, and \(X\) a scheme proper and connected over \(k\). Then the fundamental group of \(X\) is topologically of finite generation.

We proceed by induction on \(n=\dim X\), the assertion being trivial for \(n\leqslant 0\). Suppose therefore \(n>0\), and the theorem proved for the dimensions \(n'<n\). By Chow's lemma (EGA II 5.6.2) there exists a projective scheme \(X'\) over \(k\) and a surjective morphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\). One may evidently assume \(X'\) reduced, and, by passing to the normalization, normal. Thanks to the theory of descent, it suffices to prove that the fundamental groups of the connected components of \(X'\) are topologically finitely generated (IX 2). This reduces us therefore to the case where \(X\) is projective and normal. If then \(n=1\), it suffices to apply 6. If \(n\geqslant 2\), one considers a projective immersion \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{P}_k^r\), and a hyperplane section \(Y=X\cdot H\) (equipped with the induced reduced structure), such that \(Y\ne X\), i.e. \(H\not\supset X\). One will then have \(\dim Y<n\), and, taking into account the induction hypothesis, it suffices to prove that \(\pi_1(Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X)\) is surjective. But more generally:

Lemma 10

Let \(X\) be a prescheme proper over an algebraically closed field \(k\), \(g\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{P}_k^r\) a morphism. Suppose \(X\) irreducible and normal and \(\dim g(X)\geqslant 2\). Let \(H\) be a hyperplane of \(\mathbf{P}_k^r\) and \(Y=X\times_{\mathbf{P}_k^r} H\). Then \(Y\) is connected, and the homomorphism \(\pi_1(Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(X)\) is surjective.

These assertions follow indeed from the following one:

Corollary 11

Under the preceding conditions, let \(X'\) be a connected étale covering of \(X\), and \(Y'=X'\times_X Y=X'\times_{\mathbf{P}_k^r} H\) the covering induced on \(Y\). Then \(Y'\) is connected.

Since \(X\) is normal, \(X'\) is normal, hence, being connected, \(X'\) is irreducible; moreover its image in \(\mathbf{P}_k^r\) is of dimension \(\geqslant 2\). A well-known lemma due to Zariski (and called “Bertini's theorem”) implies then that if \(H_1'\) is the generic hyperplane in \(\mathbf{P}_k^r\), defined over an extension \(K\) of \(k\), then \(X'\times_{\mathbf{P}^r} H_1\) is universally irreducible, hence universally connected over \(K\). Zariski's connectedness theorem (EGA III 4) then implies that for every hyperplane \(H\) (defined over an arbitrary extension of \(k\)) \(X'\times_{\mathbf{P}^r} H\) is geometrically connected. This completes the proof of 11, hence of 9.

Corollary 12 (Lang-Serre)

Under the conditions of 9, for every finite group \(G\), the set of classes, up to isomorphism, of principal coverings of \(X\) with group \(G\), is finite.

Remark 13

Under the conditions of 10 we shall prove, when \(\dim g(X)\geqslant 3\) (at least when \(g\) is an immersion and \(X\) regular), a more precise result, known in algebraic geometry under the name of “Lefschetz's theorem”: \(\pi_1(Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X)\) is an isomorphism.5 There are in the classical cases analogous statements for the homology groups and the higher homotopy groups, which sooner or later will have to be encompassed within abstract algebraic geometry. Even for the Hodge cohomology \(\mathrm{H}^p(X,\Omega^q)\), it does not seem that the question has yet been studied; it is moreover hardly probable that for this last one, the Lefschetz theorems subsist as they stand in characteristic \(p>0\).

Remark 8

Let \(R\) be a complete discrete valuation ring, with residue field \(\mathit{k}\) algebraically closed of characteristic \(p>0\), with field of fractions \(K\) and let \(Y\) be a proper and smooth curve, connected, of genus \(g\) over \(R\). One has therefore a surjective specialization morphism \(\mathrm{sp} : \pi_1(Y_{\overline{K}}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(Y_{\mathit{k}})\). It has already been noted that if \(K\) is of characteristic 0, \(\mathrm{sp}\) is not an isomorphism as soon as \(g\geqslant1\). Suppose \(K\) of characteristic \(p\), so that \(R\) is isomorphic to the ring of formal power series \(\mathit{k}[[T]]\). In equal characteristic \(p >0\), the fundamental group is not determined by the genus \(g\), as one already sees on elliptic curves which may be ordinary or supersingular. Let us cite the recent result of A. Tamagawa (not yet published). If \(G\) is a profinite group, one denotes by \(G^{\mathrm{r\acute{e}s}}\) the profinite quotient group of \(G\), inverse limit of the topological quotients of \(G\) which are finite solvable.

Theorem (A. Tamagawa)

Suppose \(g\geqslant2\), that the special fibre \(Y_{\mathit{k}}\) is definable over a finite field and that the morphism \(\mathrm{sp}^{\mathrm{r\acute{e}s}}:\pi_1(Y_{\overline{K}})^{\mathrm{r\acute{e}s}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(Y_{\mathit{k}})^{\mathrm{r\acute{e}s}}\) deduced from \(\mathrm{sp}\) by passage to the quotient, is bijective. Then the curve \(Y\) is constant over \(R\).

Let us note that the Galois group of \(\overline{K}/K\) is solvable. The preceding statement can also be translated in the following way: suppose that every finite étale covering \(X_K\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_K\) of the generic fibre \(Y_K\), Galois with solvable Galois group, has potentially good reduction (that is to say, extends to a finite étale covering of \(Y\) after eventual replacement of \(R\) by its normalization in a finite extension of \(K\)). Then \(Y\) is constant over \(R\).

blocks.html: 2 Application of the existence theorem for sheaves: semi-continuity theorem for the fundamental groups of the fibres of a proper and separable morphism

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1

Let \(Y\) be the spectrum of a complete Noetherian local ring, with residue field \(\mathit{k}\), \(X\) a proper \(Y\)-scheme, \(X_0=X\otimes_A \mathit{k}\), \(a_0\) a geometric point of \(X_0\) and \(a\) the corresponding geometric point of \(X\). Then the canonical homomorphism \(\pi_1(X_0,a_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\) is an isomorphism.

This is merely a translation, into the language of the fundamental group, of the result recalled in IX 10. It is here that the existence theorem for sheaves in formal-algebraic geometry enters in an essential way into the theory of the fundamental group.
Let us now introduce an algebraic closure \(\overline{k}\) of the residue field \(k\), and the geometric fibre \(\overline X_0 = X_0\otimes_k \overline{k}\). We thus have the exact sequence (IX 1)

\[ e\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(\overline X_0,\overline a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X_0,a_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(k,\overline{k})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e, \]
on the other hand we have the isomorphism 1 and the analogous, more elementary, isomorphism,
\[ \pi_1(k,\overline{k})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(Y,b), \]
where \(b\) is the image of \(a\) in \(Y\). One thus finds:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

With the preceding notations, suppose \(\overline X_0=X_0\otimes_k \overline{k}\) connected, and let \(\overline a_0\) be a geometric point of \(\overline X_0\), \(a_0\) its image in \(X\), \(b_0\) its image in \(Y\). Then the following sequence of canonical homomorphisms

\[ e\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(\overline X_0,\overline a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(Y,b_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e \]

is exact.

One will compare this sequence with the exact sequence 4, but one will note that a) one did not have to make any hypothesis of flatness, or of separability on the fibres, for \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\); b) one has the important complement that the morphism \(\pi_1(\overline X_0,\overline a_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a_0)\) is injective.

This last fact will allow us to compare the fundamental group of the other geometric fibres of \(X\) over \(Y\) with that of \(\overline X_0\). Indeed, let \(y_1\) be an arbitrary point of \(Y\), \(X_1\) its fibre and \(\overline X_1\) its geometric fibre, relative to an algebraically closed extension of \(\mathit{k}(y_1)\), \(\overline a_1\) a geometric point of \(\overline X_1\), \(a_1\) its image in \(X\) and \(b_1\) its image in \(Y\). Let us choose a “path class” from \(a_1\) to \(a_0\), whence a path class from \(b_1\) to \(b_0\), whence a commutative diagram of homomorphisms:

\[ \xymatrix@=.5cm{ &{\pi_1(\overline X_1,\overline a_1)}\ar[r]&{\pi_1(X,a_1)}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{\pi_1(Y,b_1)}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{e}\\{e}\ar[r]&{\pi_1(\overline X_0,\overline a_0)}\ar[r]&{\pi_1(X,a_0)}\ar[r]&{\pi_1(Y,b_0)}\ar[r]&{e,}} \]

where the two vertical arrows written are isomorphisms. Since the second row is exact, one thus finds a canonical homomorphism, which we shall call the specialization homomorphism for the fundamental group (depending only on the chosen path class from \(a_1\) to \(a_0\), hence defined modulo inner automorphism of \(\pi_1(X,a_0)\)):
\[ \pi_1(\overline X_1,\overline a_1)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(\overline X_0,\overline a_0). \]
When the first row above is likewise exact, it follows at once that the specialization homomorphism is surjective. One thus finds, taking 4 into account:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

Under the conditions of 1, suppose moreover that the morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is separable (1) and \(\overline X_0\) connected (so that by virtue of 2 one has \(f_*(\mathscr{O}_X)=\mathscr{O}_Y\)). Then for every geometric fibre \(\overline X_1\) of \(X\) over \(Y\), equipped with a geometric point \(\overline a_1\), the specialization homomorphism defined above is a surjective homomorphism.
This is a result of semi-continuity for the fundamental group, which does not yet seem to have an analogue in algebraic topology. One may moreover state it under more general conditions:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a proper morphism with geometrically connected fibres, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian, \(y_0\) and \(y_1\) two points of \(Y\) such that \(y_0\in\overline{\{y_1\}}\), \(\overline X_0\) and \(\overline X_1\) the geometric fibres of \(X\) corresponding to given algebraically closed extensions of \(\mathit{k}(y_0)\) and \(\mathit{k}(y_1)\), \(\overline a_0\) resp. \(\overline a_1\) a geometric point of \(\overline X_0\) resp. \(\overline X_1\). Then one can define in a natural way a specialization homomorphism:

\[ \pi_1(\overline X_1,\overline a_1)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(\overline X_0,\overline a_0), \]

defined up to inner automorphism, and this is a surjective homomorphism if \(f\) is a separable morphism (1).

Indeed, it follows first from 8 that 4 is essentially independent of the algebraically closed extensions chosen for the residue fields \(\mathit{k}(y_0)\) and \(\mathit{k}(y_1)\). This allows us to replace \(Y\) by a prescheme \(Y'\) over \(Y\) having a point \(y_0'\) (resp. \(y_1'\)) above \(y_0\) (resp. \(y_1\)). One will then take for \(Y'\) the spectrum of the completion of the local ring of \(y_0\) in \(Y\), and one applies 3.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 5

The final conclusion of 4 on the surjectivity of the specialization homomorphism, and a fortiori the results 3 and 4 of which it is a consequence, becomes inexact if one no longer assumes that \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is a separable morphism, even for schemes projective over an algebraically closed field of characteristic \(0\). We shall see examples of this later on, both in the case where \(f\) is flat but where \(f\) admits a non- separable fibre (\(X\) and \(Y\) being however smooth over \(k\)), and in the case where the fibres of \(f\) are indeed separable but where \(f\) is not flat (for example \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) being a birational morphism of normal integral schemes), cf. XI 3. In these examples, it may happen that the fundamental group of the generic geometric fibre is trivial, but not that of a suitable special geometric fibre. On the other hand, even if \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is a proper separable morphism as in 4, it commonly happens that the specialization morphism is not an isomorphism. Thus, it is easy to give examples where \(\overline X_1\) is a non-singular elliptic curve, (hence its fundamental group is commutative, and its \(\ell\)-primary component for a prime number \(\ell\) prime to the characteristic is isomorphic to \(\mathbf{Z}_\ell^2\), cf. XI), while \(\overline X_0\) consists either of two rational non-singular curves meeting at two points, or of two rational curves tangent at a point, or finally of a rational curve having a singular point which is a cusp. (For the complete classification of degenerate elliptic curves, see the recent works of Kodaira [1] and Néron). One then sees that in these cases, the fundamental group of \(\overline X_0\) is respectively \(\widehat{\mathbf{Z}}\), \(e\), \(e\), hence “strictly smaller” than that of \(\overline X_1\). We shall however see later, when \(f\) is a smooth morphism, a bound on the kernel of the homomorphism of specialization, which implies in particular that if \(\mathit{k}(y_0)\) is of characteristic 0, the specialization homomorphism is an isomorphism. But even for a smooth morphism, if the characteristic of \(\mathit{k}(y_0)\) is \(>0\), it may happen that the specialization homomorphism is not an isomorphism, as one sees for example in the case where \(X\) is an abelian scheme over \(Y\) (of relative dimension \(1\), if one wishes), cf. XI 2. A satisfactory theory of the specialization of the fundamental group must take account of the “continuous component” of the “true” fundamental group, corresponding to the classification of the principal coverings with structural group the infinitesimal groups; whereby one would be entitled to expect that the “true” fundamental groups of the geometric fibres of a smooth and proper morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) form a nice local system over \(X\), an inverse limit of finite and flat group schemes over \(X\)1. We shall return later to this point of view, our present aim being on the contrary to push as far as possible the phenomena common to the topological theory and the scheme-theoretic theory of the fundamental group.

Let now \(X_0\) be a proper, smooth and connected curve of genus \(g\) over an algebraically closed field \(k\). If \(k\) is of characteristic zero, its fundamental group can be determined by transcendental means in the following way. It is known that \(X_0\) arises by base extension from a curve defined over an algebraically closed extension of finite transcendence degree of the prime field \(\mathbf{Q}\), and taking 8 into account, one may assume that \(k\) is itself of finite transcendence degree over \(\mathbf{Q}\). One may therefore assume that \(k\) is a subfield of the field \(\mathbf{C}\) of complex numbers, and a further application of 8 allows us to assume that \(k=\mathbf{C}\). It is then not difficult to verify that the fundamental group of \(X\) is isomorphic to the compactification of the fundamental group of the associated topological space \(\widetilde{X}\) (a compact oriented surface of genus \(g\)), for the topology defined by the subgroups of finite index2. It is on the other hand classical that the topological fundamental group is generated by \(2g\) generators \(s_i,t_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant g\)), subject to a single relation:
\[ (s_1t_1s_1^{-1}t_1^{-1})\cdots(s_gt_gs_g^{-1}t_g^{-1})=1. \]

Hence the fundamental group of \(X\) admits \(2g\) topological generators \(s_i,t_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant g\)), linked by the single preceding relation. If now the characteristic of \(k\) is \(p>0\), let us denote by \(A\) the ring of Witt vectors constructed with \(k\), and by \(K\) an algebraically closed extension of its field of fractions. We saw in III 4 that there exists a scheme \(X\) over \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), proper and smooth over \(Y\), reducing to \(X_0\). Applying 3 to it, one finds a surjective morphism
\[ \pi_1(X_1)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X_0), \]

where \(X_1=X\otimes_A K\). It is immediate3 that \(X_1\) is smooth over \(K\), connected (2), of dimension \(1\), and its genus is equal to \(g\) (by the invariance of the Euler-Poincaré characteristic, cf. EGA III 7). Since \(K\) is of characteristic \(0\), one may apply to it the preceding result. We have thus proved by transcendental means:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6

Let \(X_0\) be a smooth proper and connected algebraic curve over an algebraically closed field \(k\), and let \(g\) be its genus. Then \(\pi_1(X_0)\) admits a system of \(2g\) topological generators, linked by the relation written above. When the characteristic of \(k\) is \(0\), \(\pi_1(X_0)\) is even the free group of Galois type for the generators and the relation which precede.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 7

There does not exist at the present time, to the knowledge of the writer, any proof by purely algebraic means of the preceding result, (except for the genera \(0,1\)). To begin with, one hardly sees how to distinguish in \(\pi_1(X_0)\) \(2g\) elements, of which one might then expect that they form a system of topological generators. In this respect, the situation of the rational line deprived of \(n\) points, and the study of the coverings thereof tamely ramified at these points, is more agreeable, since the consideration of the ramification groups at these \(n\) points furnishes \(n\) elements of the fundamental group to be studied, of which one indeed shows that they generate topologically this fundamental group, as we shall see later on4. But even in this particularly concrete case, there does not seem to exist any purely algebraic proof. Such a proof would obviously be extremely interesting. The only fact concerning the fundamental group of a curve that one knows how to prove by purely algebraic means (with the exception of the weak finiteness theorem 12 below, proved by algebraic means by Lang-Serre [2]), seems to be the determination of the fundamental group made abelian via the Jacobian (indicated in IX 8 last line).

The last assertion of 6 is no longer valid in characteristic \(p>0\), as one already sees in the case of the elliptic curves. As we have already pointed out, we do not know whether the fundamental group of \(X_0\) is topologically of finite presentation; this seems altogether improbable.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 9

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, and \(X\) a scheme proper and connected over \(k\). Then the fundamental group of \(X\) is topologically of finite generation.

We proceed by induction on \(n=\dim X\), the assertion being trivial for \(n\leqslant 0\). Suppose therefore \(n>0\), and the theorem proved for the dimensions \(n'<n\). By Chow's lemma (EGA II 5.6.2) there exists a projective scheme \(X'\) over \(k\) and a surjective morphism \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\). One may evidently assume \(X'\) reduced, and, by passing to the normalization, normal. Thanks to the theory of descent, it suffices to prove that the fundamental groups of the connected components of \(X'\) are topologically finitely generated (IX 2). This reduces us therefore to the case where \(X\) is projective and normal. If then \(n=1\), it suffices to apply 6. If \(n\geqslant 2\), one considers a projective immersion \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{P}_k^r\), and a hyperplane section \(Y=X\cdot H\) (equipped with the induced reduced structure), such that \(Y\ne X\), i.e. \(H\not\supset X\). One will then have \(\dim Y<n\), and, taking into account the induction hypothesis, it suffices to prove that \(\pi_1(Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X)\) is surjective. But more generally:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 10

Let \(X\) be a prescheme proper over an algebraically closed field \(k\), \(g\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{P}_k^r\) a morphism. Suppose \(X\) irreducible and normal and \(\dim g(X)\geqslant 2\). Let \(H\) be a hyperplane of \(\mathbf{P}_k^r\) and \(Y=X\times_{\mathbf{P}_k^r} H\). Then \(Y\) is connected, and the homomorphism \(\pi_1(Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(X)\) is surjective.
These assertions follow indeed from the following one:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 11

Under the preceding conditions, let \(X'\) be a connected étale covering of \(X\), and \(Y'=X'\times_X Y=X'\times_{\mathbf{P}_k^r} H\) the covering induced on \(Y\). Then \(Y'\) is connected.

Since \(X\) is normal, \(X'\) is normal, hence, being connected, \(X'\) is irreducible; moreover its image in \(\mathbf{P}_k^r\) is of dimension \(\geqslant 2\). A well-known lemma due to Zariski (and called “Bertini's theorem”) implies then that if \(H_1'\) is the generic hyperplane in \(\mathbf{P}_k^r\), defined over an extension \(K\) of \(k\), then \(X'\times_{\mathbf{P}^r} H_1\) is universally irreducible, hence universally connected over \(K\). Zariski's connectedness theorem (EGA III 4) then implies that for every hyperplane \(H\) (defined over an arbitrary extension of \(k\)) \(X'\times_{\mathbf{P}^r} H\) is geometrically connected. This completes the proof of 11, hence of 9.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 12 (Lang-Serre)

Under the conditions of 9, for every finite group \(G\), the set of classes, up to isomorphism, of principal coverings of \(X\) with group \(G\), is finite.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 13

Under the conditions of 10 we shall prove, when \(\dim g(X)\geqslant 3\) (at least when \(g\) is an immersion and \(X\) regular), a more precise result, known in algebraic geometry under the name of “Lefschetz's theorem”: \(\pi_1(Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X)\) is an isomorphism.5 There are in the classical cases analogous statements for the homology groups and the higher homotopy groups, which sooner or later will have to be encompassed within abstract algebraic geometry. Even for the Hodge cohomology \(\mathrm{H}^p(X,\Omega^q)\), it does not seem that the question has yet been studied; it is moreover hardly probable that for this last one, the Lefschetz theorems subsist as they stand in characteristic \(p>0\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 8

Let \(R\) be a complete discrete valuation ring, with residue field \(\mathit{k}\) algebraically closed of characteristic \(p>0\), with field of fractions \(K\) and let \(Y\) be a proper and smooth curve, connected, of genus \(g\) over \(R\). One has therefore a surjective specialization morphism \(\mathrm{sp} : \pi_1(Y_{\overline{K}}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(Y_{\mathit{k}})\). It has already been noted that if \(K\) is of characteristic 0, \(\mathrm{sp}\) is not an isomorphism as soon as \(g\geqslant1\). Suppose \(K\) of characteristic \(p\), so that \(R\) is isomorphic to the ring of formal power series \(\mathit{k}[[T]]\). In equal characteristic \(p >0\), the fundamental group is not determined by the genus \(g\), as one already sees on elliptic curves which may be ordinary or supersingular. Let us cite the recent result of A. Tamagawa (not yet published). If \(G\) is a profinite group, one denotes by \(G^{\mathrm{r\acute{e}s}}\) the profinite quotient group of \(G\), inverse limit of the topological quotients of \(G\) which are finite solvable.

Theorem (A. Tamagawa)

Suppose \(g\geqslant2\), that the special fibre \(Y_{\mathit{k}}\) is definable over a finite field and that the morphism \(\mathrm{sp}^{\mathrm{r\acute{e}s}}:\pi_1(Y_{\overline{K}})^{\mathrm{r\acute{e}s}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(Y_{\mathit{k}})^{\mathrm{r\acute{e}s}}\) deduced from \(\mathrm{sp}\) by passage to the quotient, is bijective. Then the curve \(Y\) is constant over \(R\).

Let us note that the Galois group of \(\overline{K}/K\) is solvable. The preceding statement can also be translated in the following way: suppose that every finite étale covering \(X_K\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_K\) of the generic fibre \(Y_K\), Galois with solvable Galois group, has potentially good reduction (that is to say, extends to a finite étale covering of \(Y\) after eventual replacement of \(R\) by its normalization in a finite extension of \(K\)). Then \(Y\) is constant over \(R\).

footnotes.html:
This
extremely attractive conjecture is unfortunately proved
false by an unpublished example of M.  Artin, already when
the fibres of \(f\) are algebraic curves of given genus
\(g\geqslant 2\).

This deduction was made explicit
in one of the oral talks which were not
written up.
Cf. Exp. XII. Moreover these elements are only really determined modulo conjugation, and it is advisable to make a judicious simultaneous choice of these elements within their classes.
