following this one, theorem X 3.10.

pages.title:
Application of the purity theorem:
continuity theorem for the fundamental groups of the
fibres of a proper and smooth morphism
pages.html:
3 Application of the purity theorem: continuity theorem for the fundamental groups of the fibres of a proper and smooth morphism

Let us recall without proof1 the

Purity theorem 1 (Zariski-Nagata)

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a quasi-finite and dominant morphism of integral preschemes, with \(X\) normal, \(Y\) regular locally Noetherian, and let \(Z\) be the set of points of \(X\) where \(f\) is not étale, i.e. where \(f\) is ramified (this amounts to the same thing, I 5 (ii)). If \(Z\ne X\), \(Z\) is of codimension \(1\) in \(X\) at all its points, i.e. for every irreducible component \(Z'\) of \(Z\) with generic point \(z\), the Krull dimension of \(\mathscr{O}_{X,z}\) is equal to \(1\).

Let us recall that a prescheme is said to be normal resp. regular if its local rings are normal resp. regular, and that the relation \(Z\ne X\) also signifies that the finite extension \(R(Z)/R(X)\) (where \(R\) denotes the field of rational functions) is separable. Placing oneself at the generic point \(z\) of a component \(Z'\) of \(Z\), and localizing at the point \(y\) of \(Y\) below \(z\), one finds the equivalent statement:

Corollary 2

Let \(A\) be a regular Noetherian local ring, \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) an injective local homomorphism such that \(B\) is normal, a localization of an algebra of finite type over \(A\), and quasi-finite over \(A\); suppose moreover that \(\dim A (=\dim B)\geqslant 2\), and that for every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(B\) distinct from the maximal ideal, \(B\) is étale over \(A\) at \(\mathfrak{p}\), i.e. \(B_\mathfrak{p}\) is étale over \(A_\mathfrak{q}\) (where \(\mathfrak{q}=A\cap\mathfrak{p}\)). Then \(B\) is étale over \(A\).

Moreover, it is not difficult to reduce this last statement to the case where \(A\) is a complete local ring, hence where \(B\) is finite over \(A\). Zariski [5] gives a simple proof of this result, valid in the case of equal characteristics; the general case is due to Nagata [3], who relies on a delicate result of Chow; the latter has not been verified by any of the participants of the Seminar, and ought to be the subject of a later exposé. Let us signal only here the very simple proof in the particular case where \(\dim A=2\), which is sufficient for the most important application that we shall make of it in the present section. Since \(B\) is normal, it is a \(B\)-module of depth (old terminology: cohomological codimension) \(\geqslant 2\), hence it is an \(A\)-module of depth \(\geqslant 2\), and since \(A\) is regular of dimension \(2\), it follows that \(B\) is a free module over \(A\)2. It then follows from I 10 that the set of prime ideals \(\mathfrak{q}\) of \(A\) at which \(B\) is ramified over \(A\) is the part of \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) defined by a principal ideal (generated by the discriminant of a basis of \(B\) over \(A\)), hence is empty if it is contained in the closed point of \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), which proves 2 when \(\dim A=2\).

We shall use 1 above all in the equivalent form:

Corollary 3

Let \(X\) be a regular locally Noetherian prescheme, \(U\) an open part of \(X\) complementary to a closed part \(Z\) of \(X\) of codimension \(\geqslant 2\). Then the functor \(X'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X'\times_X U\) from the category of étale coverings of \(X\) to the category of étale coverings of \(U\) is an equivalence of categories; in particular, if \(a\) is a geometric point of \(U\), the canonical homomorphism \(\pi_1(U,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\) is an isomorphism.

The last assertion is evidently a consequence of the first, and for the latter one may evidently suppose that \(X\) is connected hence irreducible. From the normality of \(X\) it already follows that the functor \(X'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X'\times_X U\) from the category of locally free coverings (not necessarily étale) of \(X'\) to the category of coverings of \(U\) is fully faithful, for the functor \(\mathscr{E}\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}\mathscr{E}\vert U\) from the category of locally free Modules on \(X\) to the category of locally free Modules on \(U\) is so. It thus remains to prove that for every étale covering \(U'\) of \(U\), there exists an étale covering \(X'\) of \(X\) (necessarily unique by what precedes), such that \(U'\) is isomorphic to \(X'\times_X U\). One may evidently suppose \(U'\) connected, hence irreducible since (\(U\) being normal) \(U'\) is normal. Let \(K\) be the field of rational functions on \(X\), or on \(U\) (it is the same), \(K'\) that of \(U'\), then \(U'\) is identified with the normalization of \(U\) in \(K'\) I 3. Let \(X'\) be the normalization of \(X\) in \(K'\) (EGA II 6.3), then \(X'=X\times_X U \cong U'\), on the other hand \(X'\) is normal, integral, the structural morphism \(f\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is finite and dominant (for \(X\) is normal and \(K'/K\) is a finite separable extension). It is étale in \(U'=f^{-1}(U)=X'=f^{-1}(Z)\), and since \(Z\) is of codimension \(\geqslant 2\) in \(X\), \(f^{-1}(Z)\) is of codimension \(\geqslant 2\) in \(X'\). One then concludes from 1 that \(X'\) is étale over \(X\), which completes the proof.

Now let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a rational map from a locally Noetherian and regular prescheme \(X\) to a prescheme \(Y\), and suppose that \(f\) is defined in an open set \(U\) complementary to a closed part of codimension \(\geqslant 2\). Then one deduces from 3 a functor, defined up to isomorphism, from the category of étale coverings of \(Y\) to the category of étale coverings of \(X\), whence for every geometric point \(a\) of \(U\), with image \(b\) in \(Y\), a canonical homomorphism

\[ \pi_1(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(Y,b) \]

(deduced from the canonical homomorphism \(\pi_1(U,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(Y,b)\) thanks to the isomorphism \(\pi_1(U,a)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \pi_1(X,a))\). When \(f\) is a dominant morphism, \(X\) and \(Y\) being integral with fields \(K\) and \(L\), so that \(K\) is an extension of \(L\), and \(Y\) being normal, these correspondences are made precise in terms of extensions of fields by noting that for every finite extension \(L'\) of \(L\), un­ramified over \(Y\), the algebra \(K'=L'\otimes_L K\) over \(K\) is unramified over \(X\).

In particular, these reflections show that the fundamental group of connected regular locally Noetherian preschemes, pointed by geometric points localized in codimension \(\leqslant 1\), is a functor when one takes as morphisms in this category the dominant rational maps defined in complements of closed parts of codimension \(\geqslant 2\). Recalling for example that a rational map from a normal scheme over a field \(k\) to a proper scheme over \(k\) is defined in the complement of a set of codimension \(\geqslant 2\), one finds:

Corollary 4 (Birational invariance of the fundamental group)

Let \(k\) be a field, \(X\) and \(Y\) two proper schemes over \(k\) and regular, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) a birational map from \(X\) to \(Y\), \(\Omega\) an algebraically closed extension of the field of functions \(K\) of \(X\), allowing to define the fundamental group of \(X\) and the fundamental group of \(Y\). The latter are then canonically isomorphic.

This also signifies that for a finite extension \(K'\) of \(K\), if it is unramified over one proper non-singular “model” \(X\) of \(K\), it is so over every other proper non-singular model.

Remark 5

For other applications of the purity theorem, see the works of Abhyankar expounded in [4], inspired by the results of Zariski [6], proved by topological means. These latter are far from having been assimilated by “abstract” algebraic geometry and deserve renewed efforts.

We shall need a few elementary facts from ramification theory. Let \(V\) be a discrete valuation ring with field of fractions \(K\), residue field \(k\), let \(L\) be a Galois extension of \(K\) with group \(G\), \(V'\) the normalization of \(V\) in \(L\), which is a free module of rank \(n=[L{\colon }K]\) over \(V\), \(\mathfrak{m'}\) a maximal ideal of \(V'\), \(G_d\) the subgroup of \(G\) consisting of the elements leaving \(\mathfrak{m'}\) invariant, so that \(G_d\) operates on the residue extension \(k'=V'/\mathfrak{m'}\) of \(k\), and \(G_i\) the subgroup of the elements of \(G_d\) operating trivially (recall that \(G_d\) and \(G_i\) are called respectively the decomposition and inertia subgroups of \(G\)). One says that \(L\) is tamely ramified over \(V\) if \(n_i=[G_i{\colon }e]\) is of order \(p\) prime to the characteristic of \(k\) (a condition always satisfied if \(k\) is of characteristic 0). It is well known that \(G_i\) then embeds canonically into the group \({k'}^*\), and hence is isomorphic to the group of \(n_i\)-th roots of unity in \({k'}^*\), which in particular implies that \(G_i\) is cyclic. The typical case of this situation is the one where one sets \(L=K[t]/(t^n - u)\), \(u\) being a uniformizer of \(V\) and \(n\) an integer prime to \(p\): if \(K\) contains the roots of unity, \(L\) is a totally ramified Galois extension of \(K\), with Galois group \(G=G_i\) isomorphic to \(\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z}\).

Lemma 6 (Abhyankar's Lemma)

Let \(V\) be a discrete valuation ring with field of fractions \(K\), \(L\) and \(K'\) two Galois extensions of \(K\) tamely ramified over \(V\), \(n\) and \(m\) the orders of the corresponding inertia groups, \(L'\) a compositum of \(L\) and \(K'\) over \(K\). If \(m\) is a multiple of \(n\), then \(L'\) is unramified over the localizations of the normal closure \(V'\) of \(V\) in \(K'\).

Indeed, let \(W'\) be the normalization of \(V'\) in \(L'\), \(\mathfrak{m'}\) a maximal ideal of \(V'\), \(\mathfrak{n'}\) a maximal ideal of \(W'\) above \(\mathfrak{m'}\), \(\mathfrak{n}\) the maximal ideal it induces on the normalization \(W\) of \(V\) in \(L\), \(G,\ H,\ M\) the Galois groups of \(L,\ K',\ L'\) over \(K\), and \(G_i,\ H_i,\ L_i'\) the inertia groups corresponding to the chosen maximal ideals. Then \(M\) embeds into the product \(G\times H\) and \(M_i\) into the product \(G_i\times H_i\), in such a way that the projections \(M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) and \(M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H\), \(M_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_i\) and \(M_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H_i\) are surjective (sorites of the intermediate field). It already follows from this, since \(G_i\) and \(H_i\) are by hypothesis cyclic of orders \(m\) and \(n\) prime to \(p\), that \(M_i\) is of order prime to \(p\), hence cyclic, and since \(m\) is a multiple of \(n\), so that the elements of \(G_i\times H_i\) have zero \(m\)-th power, \(M_i\) is of order dividing \(m\), hence of order equal to \(m\) since \(M_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H_i\) is surjective. This last homomorphism is therefore also injective. But its kernel is the inertia group of \(\mathfrak{n'}\) above \(\mathfrak{m'}\), which proves that \(L'\) is unramified over \(K'\) at \(\mathfrak{n'}\). Whence the lemma.

Let us now place ourselves under the conditions of 4, where one has a specialization homomorphism

\[ \pi_1(\overline X_1,\overline a_1)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(\overline X_0,\overline a_0) \]

which is surjective, relative to a proper and separable morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\). We wish to make the kernel of this homomorphism precise. Proceeding as in the proof of 4, one sees that in this question, one may always suppose that \(Y\) is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring \(V\), complete and with algebraically closed residue field (for one can always find such a ring and a morphism from its spectrum \(Y'\) into \(Y\) whose image is \(\{y_0,y_1\}\)). Then one has \(X_0=\overline X_0\), \(\mathit{k}(y_0)=k=\) residue field of \(V\), \(\mathit{k}(y_1)=K=\) field of fractions of \(V\). Let \(K_s\) be the separable closure of \(K\), \(\overline K\) its algebraic closure, and for every subring \(W\) of \(\overline K\) containing \(V\), set \(X_W=X\otimes_V W\). In particular one has

\[ X_V=X, \qquad X_K=X_1, \qquad X_{\overline K}=\overline X_1. \]

Moreover the canonical morphism \(\overline X_1=X_{\overline K}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{K_s}\) induces an isomorphism on the fundamental groups (IX 11) so that, taking account of the isomorphism 1 \(\pi_1(X_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(X)\), one is reduced to studying the surjective homomorphism

\[ \pi_1(X_{K_s})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(X) \]

associated with the canonical morphism \(X_{K_s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\). The determination of the kernel of this last one amounts to the solution of the following problem: one has a connected principal covering \(Z_{K_s}\) of \(X_{K_s}\), with group \(G\) (hence associated with a homomorphism from \(\pi_1(X_{K_s})\) into \(G\)), determine under what conditions it is isomorphic to the inverse image of a principal covering \(Z\) of \(X\) with group \(G\).

Let us first note that \(K_s\) is the increasing filtered union of its finite subextensions \(K'\) over \(K\), and that consequently, \(Z_{K_s}\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of a principal covering \(Z_{K'}\) of \(X_{K'}\) for a suitable \(K'\) (one will however take care that for fixed \(K'\), \(Z_{K'}\) is not determined uniquely). To say that \(Z_{K_s}\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of a principal covering \(Z\) of \(X\) means that there exists a finite subextension \(K''\supset K'\) of \(K_s\) such that \(Z_{K''}=Z_{K'}\otimes_{K'} K''\) is isomorphic to \(Z\otimes_V K''\). Let us now denote, for a finite subextension \(K'\) of \(K_s\), by \(V'\) the normalization of \(V\) in \(K'\), which is a discrete valuation ring, complete, with residue field \(k\). Hence the canonical morphism \(X_{V'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_V\) induces an isomorphism for the fibres above the closed points of \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(V)\) and \(Y'=\operatorname{Spec}(V')\) and it then follows from 1 applied to \(X_V\) and \(X_{V'}\) that the homomorphism induced for the fundamental groups \(\pi_1(X_{V'})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X_V)\) is an isomorphism, or again that every principal covering of \(X_{V'}\) is the inverse image of a principal covering of \(X_V\) determined up to isomorphism. This therefore implies the

Lemma 7

Let \(Z_{K'}\) be a connected principal covering of \(X_{K'}\) with group \(G\), \(Z_{K}\) its inverse image on \(X_{K_s}\). In order that this last one be isomorphic to the inverse image of a principal covering \(Z\) of \(X\), it is necessary and sufficient that there exist a finite extension \(K''\supset K'\) of \(K\) in \(K_s\) such that the principal covering \(Z_{K''}\) of \(X_{K''}\) be induced by a principal covering of \(X_{V''}\).

Suppose in particular that the \(X_{V''}\) are normal (it suffices for example for this that \(X_0\) be normal, and a fortiori that \(X_0\) be smooth, cf. I 1). Since they are connected, they are therefore irreducible. Let \(L\) be the field of rational functions for \(X\) and \(X_{K}\), \(L'\) that for \(X_{V'}\) and \(X_{K'}\), \(L''\) that for \(X_{V''}\) and \(X_{K''}\). Then under the conditions of 7, \(Z_{K'}\) defines a finite separable extension \(R'\) of \(L'\), and \(Z_{K''}\) defines the extension \(R''=R'\otimes_{L'} L'' = R'\otimes_{K'} K''\). The condition envisaged in 7 therefore also means that there exists a finite separable extension \(K''\) of \(K'\) such that \(R''=R'\otimes_{K'} K''\) is unramified above the normal scheme \(X_{V''}\) with field \(L''=L'\otimes_{K'} K''\), and not only above the open part \(X_{K''}\) of \(X_{V''}\).

We suppose henceforth that \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is a smooth morphism, so that the morphisms \(X_{V'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec}(V')\) are smooth, so that the schemes \(X_{V'}\) are regular. Note that the fibre of the closed point of \(\operatorname{Spec}(V')\) in \(X_{V'}\) is irreducible and of codimension \(1\). Let \(\mathfrak{o'}\) be its local ring, which is thus a discrete valuation ring with field \(L'\), with residue field isomorphic to the field of rational functions of \(X_0\), hence having the same characteristic as \(k\). Let us define likewise \(\mathfrak{o''}\) in \(L''\), which is evidently the normalization of \(\mathfrak{o'}\) in \(L''\). It then follows from the purity theorem 1 or 3 that in order that \(R''\) be unramified over \(X_{V''}\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(R''\) be unramified over \(\mathfrak{o''}\), the normalization of \(\mathfrak{o'}\) in \(L''\).

Let us note now that if \(u'\) is a uniformizer of \(V'\), it is also a uniformizer of \(\mathfrak{o'}\). If then \(n\) is an integer prime to the characteristic \(p\) of \(k\), and if we take \(K''=K'[t]/(t^n-u)\), then \(K''\) is a finite Galois extension of \(K'\) and \(L''\) is isomorphic to \(L'[t]/(t^n-u')\), hence is tamely ramified over \(\mathfrak{o'}\) and with inertia group of order \(n\). Suppose then that \(G\) is of order prime to \(p\), which implies that \(R'\) is tamely ramified over \(\mathfrak{o'}\), and take for \(n\) a multiple prime to \(p\) of the order of the inertia group of \(R'\) over \(\mathfrak{o'}\) (for example \(n=[G\colon e]\)). Applying the lemma of Abhyankar 6, we see that the condition envisaged in 7 is satisfied.

This proves the following theorem:

Theorem 8

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a proper and smooth morphism, with geometrically connected fibres, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian, \(y_0\) and \(y_1\) two points of \(Y\) such that \(y_0\in\overline{\{y_1\}}\), \(\overline{X}_0\) and \(\overline{X}_1\) the corresponding geometric fibres, consider the specialization homomorphism (4) \(\pi_1(\overline X_1)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(\overline X_0)\). This homomorphism is surjective, and every continuous homomorphism of \(\pi_1(\overline X_1)\) into a finite group \(G\) of order prime to the characteristic \(p\) of \(\mathit{k}(y_0)\) comes from a homomorphism of \(\pi_1(\overline{X}_0)\) into \(G\).

In other words:

Corollary 9

If \(\mathit{k}(y_0)\) is of characteristic zero, then the specialization homomorphism is an isomorphism. If \(\mathit{k}(y_0)\) is of characteristic \(p>0\), then the kernel of the specialization homomorphism is contained in the intersection of the kernels of the continuous homomorphisms of \(\pi_1(\overline X_1)\) into finite groups of order prime to \(p\) (or again, the closed invariant subgroup generated by a Sylow \(p\)-subgroup of the group of Galois type \(\pi_1(\overline X_1)\)); if therefore \(\pi_1(\overline X_1)^{(p)}\) denotes the quotient group of \(\pi_1(\overline X_1)\) by the preceding closed subgroup, and if we define \(\pi_1(\overline X_0)^{(p)}\) in the same way, then the specialization homomorphism induces an isomorphism

\[ \pi_1(\overline X_1)^{(p)}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \pi_1(\overline X_0)^{(p)} \]

It will be noted that the proof of 8 is purely algebraic. Proceeding as in 6, we conclude from it by transcendental means:

Corollary 10

Let \(X_0\) be a proper, smooth and connected curve of genus \(g\) over an algebraically closed field of characteristic \(p\). With the notation introduced in 9, the group \(\pi_1(X_0)^{(p)}\) is isomorphic to \(\Gamma^{(p)}\), where \(\Gamma\) is the group of Galois type generated by generators \(s_i,~t_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant g\)) linked by the relation

\[ (s_1t_1s_1^{-1}t_1^{-1})\cdots (s_gt_gs_g^{-1}t_g^{-1})=1 \]
Remarks 11

In the case where \(\mathit{k}(y_0)\) is of characteristic zero, the result 9 is well known by transcendental means. It will be noted that the proof of 10 makes appeal to the purity theorem in the case of unequal characteristics, but only in the case of rings of dimension 2, where the proof of the said theorem is easy and has been recalled in the text.

[6]

blocks.html: 3 Application of the purity theorem: continuity theorem for the fundamental groups of the fibres of a proper and smooth morphism
Let us recall without proof1 the

blocks.title: Purity theorem
blocks.html:
Purity theorem 1 (Zariski-Nagata)

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a quasi-finite and dominant morphism of integral preschemes, with \(X\) normal, \(Y\) regular locally Noetherian, and let \(Z\) be the set of points of \(X\) where \(f\) is not étale, i.e. where \(f\) is ramified (this amounts to the same thing, I 5 (ii)). If \(Z\ne X\), \(Z\) is of codimension \(1\) in \(X\) at all its points, i.e. for every irreducible component \(Z'\) of \(Z\) with generic point \(z\), the Krull dimension of \(\mathscr{O}_{X,z}\) is equal to \(1\).

Let us recall that a prescheme is said to be normal resp. regular if its local rings are normal resp. regular, and that the relation \(Z\ne X\) also signifies that the finite extension \(R(Z)/R(X)\) (where \(R\) denotes the field of rational functions) is separable. Placing oneself at the generic point \(z\) of a component \(Z'\) of \(Z\), and localizing at the point \(y\) of \(Y\) below \(z\), one finds the equivalent statement:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

Let \(A\) be a regular Noetherian local ring, \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) an injective local homomorphism such that \(B\) is normal, a localization of an algebra of finite type over \(A\), and quasi-finite over \(A\); suppose moreover that \(\dim A (=\dim B)\geqslant 2\), and that for every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(B\) distinct from the maximal ideal, \(B\) is étale over \(A\) at \(\mathfrak{p}\), i.e. \(B_\mathfrak{p}\) is étale over \(A_\mathfrak{q}\) (where \(\mathfrak{q}=A\cap\mathfrak{p}\)). Then \(B\) is étale over \(A\).

Moreover, it is not difficult to reduce this last statement to the case where \(A\) is a complete local ring, hence where \(B\) is finite over \(A\). Zariski [5] gives a simple proof of this result, valid in the case of equal characteristics; the general case is due to Nagata [3], who relies on a delicate result of Chow; the latter has not been verified by any of the participants of the Seminar, and ought to be the subject of a later exposé. Let us signal only here the very simple proof in the particular case where \(\dim A=2\), which is sufficient for the most important application that we shall make of it in the present section. Since \(B\) is normal, it is a \(B\)-module of depth (old terminology: cohomological codimension) \(\geqslant 2\), hence it is an \(A\)-module of depth \(\geqslant 2\), and since \(A\) is regular of dimension \(2\), it follows that \(B\) is a free module over \(A\)2. It then follows from I 10 that the set of prime ideals \(\mathfrak{q}\) of \(A\) at which \(B\) is ramified over \(A\) is the part of \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) defined by a principal ideal (generated by the discriminant of a basis of \(B\) over \(A\)), hence is empty if it is contained in the closed point of \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), which proves 2 when \(\dim A=2\).
We shall use 1 above all in the equivalent form:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

Let \(X\) be a regular locally Noetherian prescheme, \(U\) an open part of \(X\) complementary to a closed part \(Z\) of \(X\) of codimension \(\geqslant 2\). Then the functor \(X'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X'\times_X U\) from the category of étale coverings of \(X\) to the category of étale coverings of \(U\) is an equivalence of categories; in particular, if \(a\) is a geometric point of \(U\), the canonical homomorphism \(\pi_1(U,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a)\) is an isomorphism.

The last assertion is evidently a consequence of the first, and for the latter one may evidently suppose that \(X\) is connected hence irreducible. From the normality of \(X\) it already follows that the functor \(X'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} X'\times_X U\) from the category of locally free coverings (not necessarily étale) of \(X'\) to the category of coverings of \(U\) is fully faithful, for the functor \(\mathscr{E}\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}\mathscr{E}\vert U\) from the category of locally free Modules on \(X\) to the category of locally free Modules on \(U\) is so. It thus remains to prove that for every étale covering \(U'\) of \(U\), there exists an étale covering \(X'\) of \(X\) (necessarily unique by what precedes), such that \(U'\) is isomorphic to \(X'\times_X U\). One may evidently suppose \(U'\) connected, hence irreducible since (\(U\) being normal) \(U'\) is normal. Let \(K\) be the field of rational functions on \(X\), or on \(U\) (it is the same), \(K'\) that of \(U'\), then \(U'\) is identified with the normalization of \(U\) in \(K'\) I 3. Let \(X'\) be the normalization of \(X\) in \(K'\) (EGA II 6.3), then \(X'=X\times_X U \cong U'\), on the other hand \(X'\) is normal, integral, the structural morphism \(f\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is finite and dominant (for \(X\) is normal and \(K'/K\) is a finite separable extension). It is étale in \(U'=f^{-1}(U)=X'=f^{-1}(Z)\), and since \(Z\) is of codimension \(\geqslant 2\) in \(X\), \(f^{-1}(Z)\) is of codimension \(\geqslant 2\) in \(X'\). One then concludes from 1 that \(X'\) is étale over \(X\), which completes the proof.

Now let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a rational map from a locally Noetherian and regular prescheme \(X\) to a prescheme \(Y\), and suppose that \(f\) is defined in an open set \(U\) complementary to a closed part of codimension \(\geqslant 2\). Then one deduces from 3 a functor, defined up to isomorphism, from the category of étale coverings of \(Y\) to the category of étale coverings of \(X\), whence for every geometric point \(a\) of \(U\), with image \(b\) in \(Y\), a canonical homomorphism
\[ \pi_1(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(Y,b) \]

(deduced from the canonical homomorphism \(\pi_1(U,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(Y,b)\) thanks to the isomorphism \(\pi_1(U,a)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \pi_1(X,a))\). When \(f\) is a dominant morphism, \(X\) and \(Y\) being integral with fields \(K\) and \(L\), so that \(K\) is an extension of \(L\), and \(Y\) being normal, these correspondences are made precise in terms of extensions of fields by noting that for every finite extension \(L'\) of \(L\), un­ramified over \(Y\), the algebra \(K'=L'\otimes_L K\) over \(K\) is unramified over \(X\).

In particular, these reflections show that the fundamental group of connected regular locally Noetherian preschemes, pointed by geometric points localized in codimension \(\leqslant 1\), is a functor when one takes as morphisms in this category the dominant rational maps defined in complements of closed parts of codimension \(\geqslant 2\). Recalling for example that a rational map from a normal scheme over a field \(k\) to a proper scheme over \(k\) is defined in the complement of a set of codimension \(\geqslant 2\), one finds:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4 (Birational invariance of the fundamental group)

Let \(k\) be a field, \(X\) and \(Y\) two proper schemes over \(k\) and regular, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) a birational map from \(X\) to \(Y\), \(\Omega\) an algebraically closed extension of the field of functions \(K\) of \(X\), allowing to define the fundamental group of \(X\) and the fundamental group of \(Y\). The latter are then canonically isomorphic.
This also signifies that for a finite extension \(K'\) of \(K\), if it is unramified over one proper non-singular “model” \(X\) of \(K\), it is so over every other proper non-singular model.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 5

For other applications of the purity theorem, see the works of Abhyankar expounded in [4], inspired by the results of Zariski [6], proved by topological means. These latter are far from having been assimilated by “abstract” algebraic geometry and deserve renewed efforts.

We shall need a few elementary facts from ramification theory. Let \(V\) be a discrete valuation ring with field of fractions \(K\), residue field \(k\), let \(L\) be a Galois extension of \(K\) with group \(G\), \(V'\) the normalization of \(V\) in \(L\), which is a free module of rank \(n=[L{\colon }K]\) over \(V\), \(\mathfrak{m'}\) a maximal ideal of \(V'\), \(G_d\) the subgroup of \(G\) consisting of the elements leaving \(\mathfrak{m'}\) invariant, so that \(G_d\) operates on the residue extension \(k'=V'/\mathfrak{m'}\) of \(k\), and \(G_i\) the subgroup of the elements of \(G_d\) operating trivially (recall that \(G_d\) and \(G_i\) are called respectively the decomposition and inertia subgroups of \(G\)). One says that \(L\) is tamely ramified over \(V\) if \(n_i=[G_i{\colon }e]\) is of order \(p\) prime to the characteristic of \(k\) (a condition always satisfied if \(k\) is of characteristic 0). It is well known that \(G_i\) then embeds canonically into the group \({k'}^*\), and hence is isomorphic to the group of \(n_i\)-th roots of unity in \({k'}^*\), which in particular implies that \(G_i\) is cyclic. The typical case of this situation is the one where one sets \(L=K[t]/(t^n - u)\), \(u\) being a uniformizer of \(V\) and \(n\) an integer prime to \(p\): if \(K\) contains the roots of unity, \(L\) is a totally ramified Galois extension of \(K\), with Galois group \(G=G_i\) isomorphic to \(\mathbf{Z}/n\mathbf{Z}\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6 (Abhyankar's Lemma)

Let \(V\) be a discrete valuation ring with field of fractions \(K\), \(L\) and \(K'\) two Galois extensions of \(K\) tamely ramified over \(V\), \(n\) and \(m\) the orders of the corresponding inertia groups, \(L'\) a compositum of \(L\) and \(K'\) over \(K\). If \(m\) is a multiple of \(n\), then \(L'\) is unramified over the localizations of the normal closure \(V'\) of \(V\) in \(K'\).

Indeed, let \(W'\) be the normalization of \(V'\) in \(L'\), \(\mathfrak{m'}\) a maximal ideal of \(V'\), \(\mathfrak{n'}\) a maximal ideal of \(W'\) above \(\mathfrak{m'}\), \(\mathfrak{n}\) the maximal ideal it induces on the normalization \(W\) of \(V\) in \(L\), \(G,\ H,\ M\) the Galois groups of \(L,\ K',\ L'\) over \(K\), and \(G_i,\ H_i,\ L_i'\) the inertia groups corresponding to the chosen maximal ideals. Then \(M\) embeds into the product \(G\times H\) and \(M_i\) into the product \(G_i\times H_i\), in such a way that the projections \(M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) and \(M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H\), \(M_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_i\) and \(M_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H_i\) are surjective (sorites of the intermediate field). It already follows from this, since \(G_i\) and \(H_i\) are by hypothesis cyclic of orders \(m\) and \(n\) prime to \(p\), that \(M_i\) is of order prime to \(p\), hence cyclic, and since \(m\) is a multiple of \(n\), so that the elements of \(G_i\times H_i\) have zero \(m\)-th power, \(M_i\) is of order dividing \(m\), hence of order equal to \(m\) since \(M_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H_i\) is surjective. This last homomorphism is therefore also injective. But its kernel is the inertia group of \(\mathfrak{n'}\) above \(\mathfrak{m'}\), which proves that \(L'\) is unramified over \(K'\) at \(\mathfrak{n'}\). Whence the lemma.
Let us now place ourselves under the conditions of 4, where one has a specialization homomorphism
\[ \pi_1(\overline X_1,\overline a_1)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(\overline X_0,\overline a_0) \]

which is surjective, relative to a proper and separable morphism \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\). We wish to make the kernel of this homomorphism precise. Proceeding as in the proof of 4, one sees that in this question, one may always suppose that \(Y\) is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring \(V\), complete and with algebraically closed residue field (for one can always find such a ring and a morphism from its spectrum \(Y'\) into \(Y\) whose image is \(\{y_0,y_1\}\)). Then one has \(X_0=\overline X_0\), \(\mathit{k}(y_0)=k=\) residue field of \(V\), \(\mathit{k}(y_1)=K=\) field of fractions of \(V\). Let \(K_s\) be the separable closure of \(K\), \(\overline K\) its algebraic closure, and for every subring \(W\) of \(\overline K\) containing \(V\), set \(X_W=X\otimes_V W\). In particular one has
\[ X_V=X, \qquad X_K=X_1, \qquad X_{\overline K}=\overline X_1. \]

Moreover the canonical morphism \(\overline X_1=X_{\overline K}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{K_s}\) induces an isomorphism on the fundamental groups (IX 11) so that, taking account of the isomorphism 1 \(\pi_1(X_0)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(X)\), one is reduced to studying the surjective homomorphism
\[ \pi_1(X_{K_s})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(X) \]

associated with the canonical morphism \(X_{K_s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\). The determination of the kernel of this last one amounts to the solution of the following problem: one has a connected principal covering \(Z_{K_s}\) of \(X_{K_s}\), with group \(G\) (hence associated with a homomorphism from \(\pi_1(X_{K_s})\) into \(G\)), determine under what conditions it is isomorphic to the inverse image of a principal covering \(Z\) of \(X\) with group \(G\).

Let us first note that \(K_s\) is the increasing filtered union of its finite subextensions \(K'\) over \(K\), and that consequently, \(Z_{K_s}\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of a principal covering \(Z_{K'}\) of \(X_{K'}\) for a suitable \(K'\) (one will however take care that for fixed \(K'\), \(Z_{K'}\) is not determined uniquely). To say that \(Z_{K_s}\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of a principal covering \(Z\) of \(X\) means that there exists a finite subextension \(K''\supset K'\) of \(K_s\) such that \(Z_{K''}=Z_{K'}\otimes_{K'} K''\) is isomorphic to \(Z\otimes_V K''\). Let us now denote, for a finite subextension \(K'\) of \(K_s\), by \(V'\) the normalization of \(V\) in \(K'\), which is a discrete valuation ring, complete, with residue field \(k\). Hence the canonical morphism \(X_{V'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_V\) induces an isomorphism for the fibres above the closed points of \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(V)\) and \(Y'=\operatorname{Spec}(V')\) and it then follows from 1 applied to \(X_V\) and \(X_{V'}\) that the homomorphism induced for the fundamental groups \(\pi_1(X_{V'})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X_V)\) is an isomorphism, or again that every principal covering of \(X_{V'}\) is the inverse image of a principal covering of \(X_V\) determined up to isomorphism. This therefore implies the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7

Let \(Z_{K'}\) be a connected principal covering of \(X_{K'}\) with group \(G\), \(Z_{K}\) its inverse image on \(X_{K_s}\). In order that this last one be isomorphic to the inverse image of a principal covering \(Z\) of \(X\), it is necessary and sufficient that there exist a finite extension \(K''\supset K'\) of \(K\) in \(K_s\) such that the principal covering \(Z_{K''}\) of \(X_{K''}\) be induced by a principal covering of \(X_{V''}\).

Suppose in particular that the \(X_{V''}\) are normal (it suffices for example for this that \(X_0\) be normal, and a fortiori that \(X_0\) be smooth, cf. I 1). Since they are connected, they are therefore irreducible. Let \(L\) be the field of rational functions for \(X\) and \(X_{K}\), \(L'\) that for \(X_{V'}\) and \(X_{K'}\), \(L''\) that for \(X_{V''}\) and \(X_{K''}\). Then under the conditions of 7, \(Z_{K'}\) defines a finite separable extension \(R'\) of \(L'\), and \(Z_{K''}\) defines the extension \(R''=R'\otimes_{L'} L'' = R'\otimes_{K'} K''\). The condition envisaged in 7 therefore also means that there exists a finite separable extension \(K''\) of \(K'\) such that \(R''=R'\otimes_{K'} K''\) is unramified above the normal scheme \(X_{V''}\) with field \(L''=L'\otimes_{K'} K''\), and not only above the open part \(X_{K''}\) of \(X_{V''}\).

We suppose henceforth that \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is a smooth morphism, so that the morphisms \(X_{V'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec}(V')\) are smooth, so that the schemes \(X_{V'}\) are regular. Note that the fibre of the closed point of \(\operatorname{Spec}(V')\) in \(X_{V'}\) is irreducible and of codimension \(1\). Let \(\mathfrak{o'}\) be its local ring, which is thus a discrete valuation ring with field \(L'\), with residue field isomorphic to the field of rational functions of \(X_0\), hence having the same characteristic as \(k\). Let us define likewise \(\mathfrak{o''}\) in \(L''\), which is evidently the normalization of \(\mathfrak{o'}\) in \(L''\). It then follows from the purity theorem 1 or 3 that in order that \(R''\) be unramified over \(X_{V''}\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(R''\) be unramified over \(\mathfrak{o''}\), the normalization of \(\mathfrak{o'}\) in \(L''\).

Let us note now that if \(u'\) is a uniformizer of \(V'\), it is also a uniformizer of \(\mathfrak{o'}\). If then \(n\) is an integer prime to the characteristic \(p\) of \(k\), and if we take \(K''=K'[t]/(t^n-u)\), then \(K''\) is a finite Galois extension of \(K'\) and \(L''\) is isomorphic to \(L'[t]/(t^n-u')\), hence is tamely ramified over \(\mathfrak{o'}\) and with inertia group of order \(n\). Suppose then that \(G\) is of order prime to \(p\), which implies that \(R'\) is tamely ramified over \(\mathfrak{o'}\), and take for \(n\) a multiple prime to \(p\) of the order of the inertia group of \(R'\) over \(\mathfrak{o'}\) (for example \(n=[G\colon e]\)). Applying the lemma of Abhyankar 6, we see that the condition envisaged in 7 is satisfied.
This proves the following theorem:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 8

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a proper and smooth morphism, with geometrically connected fibres, with \(Y\) locally Noetherian, \(y_0\) and \(y_1\) two points of \(Y\) such that \(y_0\in\overline{\{y_1\}}\), \(\overline{X}_0\) and \(\overline{X}_1\) the corresponding geometric fibres, consider the specialization homomorphism (4) \(\pi_1(\overline X_1)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(\overline X_0)\). This homomorphism is surjective, and every continuous homomorphism of \(\pi_1(\overline X_1)\) into a finite group \(G\) of order prime to the characteristic \(p\) of \(\mathit{k}(y_0)\) comes from a homomorphism of \(\pi_1(\overline{X}_0)\) into \(G\).
In other words:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9

If \(\mathit{k}(y_0)\) is of characteristic zero, then the specialization homomorphism is an isomorphism. If \(\mathit{k}(y_0)\) is of characteristic \(p>0\), then the kernel of the specialization homomorphism is contained in the intersection of the kernels of the continuous homomorphisms of \(\pi_1(\overline X_1)\) into finite groups of order prime to \(p\) (or again, the closed invariant subgroup generated by a Sylow \(p\)-subgroup of the group of Galois type \(\pi_1(\overline X_1)\)); if therefore \(\pi_1(\overline X_1)^{(p)}\) denotes the quotient group of \(\pi_1(\overline X_1)\) by the preceding closed subgroup, and if we define \(\pi_1(\overline X_0)^{(p)}\) in the same way, then the specialization homomorphism induces an isomorphism

\[ \pi_1(\overline X_1)^{(p)}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \pi_1(\overline X_0)^{(p)} \]
It will be noted that the proof of 8 is purely algebraic. Proceeding as in 6, we conclude from it by transcendental means:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 10

Let \(X_0\) be a proper, smooth and connected curve of genus \(g\) over an algebraically closed field of characteristic \(p\). With the notation introduced in 9, the group \(\pi_1(X_0)^{(p)}\) is isomorphic to \(\Gamma^{(p)}\), where \(\Gamma\) is the group of Galois type generated by generators \(s_i,~t_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant g\)) linked by the relation

\[ (s_1t_1s_1^{-1}t_1^{-1})\cdots (s_gt_gs_g^{-1}t_g^{-1})=1 \]

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 11

In the case where \(\mathit{k}(y_0)\) is of characteristic zero, the result 9 is well known by transcendental means. It will be noted that the proof of 10 makes appeal to the purity theorem in the case of unequal characteristics, but only in the case of rings of dimension 2, where the proof of the said theorem is easy and has been recalled in the text.
